title: Comparative & Superlative Mistakes - 50 Mistakes
definition: Comparatives (comparing two things - taller, more beautiful) and superlatives (comparing three or more - tallest, most beautiful) each follow specific formation rules depending on the length of the adjective. Here are 50 common mistakes with these forms, explained simply, with the correct rule to apply.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is more taller than me.
commonMistakes.right: She is taller than me.
commonMistakes.why: 'Taller' already includes the comparative ending '-er', so adding 'more' in front creates a double comparative, which is incorrect. For short, one-syllable adjectives, add just '-er' - never combine it with 'more'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most tallest building.
commonMistakes.right: This is the tallest building.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the comparative mistake, 'tallest' already has the superlative ending '-est', so adding 'most' in front doubles up unnecessarily. For short adjectives, use just '-est' alone for the superlative, never with 'most' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is the most smartest student.
commonMistakes.right: He is the smartest student.
commonMistakes.why: 'Smartest' already carries the superlative meaning through its ending, so 'most' in front repeats the same function. Remember this general rule: never combine 'most/more' with an adjective that already ends in '-er' or '-est'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is more better than that.
commonMistakes.right: This is better than that.
commonMistakes.why: 'Better' is already the comparative form of 'good' (an irregular form), so adding 'more' in front is redundant. Irregular comparatives like 'better' and 'worse' never need 'more' added to them.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most best option.
commonMistakes.right: This is the best option.
commonMistakes.why: 'Best' is already the superlative form of 'good', so 'most' in front repeats the same idea unnecessarily. Just like 'better', irregular superlatives like 'best' and 'worst' never take 'most'.

commonMistakes.wrong: She sings more beautifuller than him.
commonMistakes.right: She sings more beautifully than him.
commonMistakes.why: 'Beautifully' is a longer word, so it needs 'more' in front rather than an '-er' ending directly attached - 'beautifuller' isn't a real word. Remember: longer adjectives and adverbs (usually two or more syllables) use 'more/most', while short ones use '-er/-est'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the beautifullest place I've seen.
commonMistakes.right: This is the most beautiful place I've seen.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'beautiful' has three syllables, it needs 'most' in front for the superlative, not an '-est' ending directly attached. Longer words like beautiful, intelligent, and important always use 'most', never a direct '-est' ending.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is more intelligenter than his brother.
commonMistakes.right: He is more intelligent than his brother.
commonMistakes.why: 'Intelligent' is a long word (four syllables), so it only needs 'more' added, not an additional '-er' ending on top. Remember: long words get 'more/most' alone, with no extra ending added to the word itself.

commonMistakes.wrong: This road is more wide than that one.
commonMistakes.right: This road is wider than that one.
commonMistakes.why: 'Wide' is a short, one-syllable word, so it should take the '-er' ending directly (wider), not 'more' in front. Short adjectives (one syllable, sometimes two) take '-er/-est' endings rather than 'more/most'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is more easy than I expected.
commonMistakes.right: This is easier than I expected.
commonMistakes.why: 'Easy' is a two-syllable word ending in 'y', which usually takes '-er' (with the y changed to i) rather than 'more'. For two-syllable words ending in 'y' (happy, easy, busy), change the y to i and add '-er/-est'.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is the most happiest person I know.
commonMistakes.right: She is the happiest person I know.
commonMistakes.why: 'Happiest' already contains the superlative form (with y changed to i, plus -est), so 'most' in front is unnecessary repetition. This follows the same y-to-i rule as 'easier/easiest', without needing 'most' added.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is more funnier than his friend.
commonMistakes.right: He is funnier than his friend.
commonMistakes.why: 'Funny' follows the same two-syllable, y-ending pattern as 'happy' and 'easy' - it takes '-er' directly (funnier), not 'more'. Keep grouping these y-ending, two-syllable words together in memory since they all follow the same rule.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most simplest solution.
commonMistakes.right: This is the simplest solution.
commonMistakes.why: 'Simplest' already carries the superlative meaning through its ending, making 'most' redundant. Even though 'simple' has two syllables, it's one of the exceptions that commonly takes '-er/-est' rather than 'more/most' - so no 'most' is needed here.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is more prettier than her sister.
commonMistakes.right: She is prettier than her sister.
commonMistakes.why: 'Pretty' follows the same y-ending, two-syllable rule as happy and easy - it becomes 'prettier' directly, without needing 'more' added. Practice recognizing this specific word pattern: two syllables ending in 'y' = change to 'i' + '-er/-est'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the goodest movie I've seen.
commonMistakes.right: This is the best movie I've seen.
commonMistakes.why: 'Good' is an irregular adjective - its comparative and superlative forms are completely different words (better, best), not simply 'gooder' or 'goodest'. Memorize irregular forms separately since they don't follow the normal rules: good-better-best, bad-worse-worst.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is badder than I thought.
commonMistakes.right: This is worse than I thought.
commonMistakes.why: 'Bad' is irregular, and its comparative form is 'worse' (not 'badder'), just as 'good' becomes 'better'. Keep memorizing this irregular pair alongside good/better/best: bad-worse-worst.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the baddest experience ever.
commonMistakes.right: This is the worst experience ever.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'badder', 'baddest' isn't a standard word - the correct superlative of 'bad' is 'worst'. Complete the irregular set: bad, worse, worst - none of these add a regular ending.

commonMistakes.wrong: I have less money than more people think.
commonMistakes.right: I have less money than most people think.
commonMistakes.why: This sentence confuses 'more' (extra than) with 'most' (the majority of) - since the intended meaning is 'the majority of people', 'most' is correct here, not 'more'. Double-check whether you mean 'additional amount' (more) or 'the majority' (most) before choosing.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is far more better than the last one.
commonMistakes.right: This is far better than the last one.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'better' is already the correct comparative form, adding 'more' creates the same double comparative mistake covered earlier - 'far' alone is enough to add emphasis. Use intensifiers like 'far', 'much', or 'a lot' before an already-correct comparative, but never add 'more' as well.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is the more talented singer in the group.
commonMistakes.right: She is the most talented singer in the group.
commonMistakes.why: Since this compares her to the WHOLE group (three or more people), the superlative 'most talented' is needed, not the comparative 'more talented' (which is only for comparing two things). Check whether you're comparing two things (comparative) or three or more (superlative) before choosing your form.

commonMistakes.wrong: Between the two books, this is the best one.
commonMistakes.right: Between the two books, this is the better one.
commonMistakes.why: Since only TWO things are being compared here ('between the two'), the comparative form 'better' is correct, not the superlative 'best' (which is reserved for three or more things). Remember: comparative for two things, superlative for three or more - 'between' often signals exactly two.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is the taller person in our whole class.
commonMistakes.right: He is the tallest person in our whole class.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'our whole class' includes many people (more than two), the superlative 'tallest' is needed, not the comparative 'taller'. Whenever you're comparing someone to a large group, use the superlative form, not comparative.

commonMistakes.wrong: This cake tastes gooder than the last one.
commonMistakes.right: This cake tastes better than the last one.
commonMistakes.why: This repeats the irregular good/better/best mistake - 'gooder' is never correct; always use 'better' for comparative. This irregular pattern is worth practicing until it's completely automatic, since it's one of the most commonly used comparatives.

commonMistakes.wrong: She runs more faster than anyone else.
commonMistakes.right: She runs faster than anyone else.
commonMistakes.why: 'Fast' is a short, one-syllable word, so it takes '-er' directly (faster), and adding 'more' creates a double comparative. Keep practicing this rule with short adjectives and adverbs: one syllable = '-er/-est' only.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most fastest car in the race.
commonMistakes.right: This is the fastest car in the race.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'faster', 'fastest' already carries the superlative meaning, so 'most' in front is unnecessary repetition. This follows the exact same rule as tallest, smartest, and other short-word superlatives.

commonMistakes.wrong: My score was more high than his.
commonMistakes.right: My score was higher than his.
commonMistakes.why: 'High' is a short, one-syllable word, so it takes '-er' directly (higher), not 'more' in front. Continue reinforcing the short-word rule: one syllable adjectives get '-er/-est', not 'more/most'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most highest score ever recorded.
commonMistakes.right: This is the highest score ever recorded.
commonMistakes.why: 'Highest' already contains the superlative ending, making 'most' redundant when added in front. This is the same pattern seen with tallest, fastest, and smartest - all short adjectives forming their own superlative directly.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is more strong than he looks.
commonMistakes.right: He is stronger than he looks.
commonMistakes.why: 'Strong' is a short, one-syllable word, so it takes '-er' directly (stronger), not 'more'. Keep applying the same consistent rule across all short adjectives you encounter.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most strongest metal known.
commonMistakes.right: This is the strongest metal known.
commonMistakes.why: Just as before, 'strongest' already has the superlative ending built in, so 'most' is unnecessary. By now, this pattern should feel very familiar across many different short adjectives.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is more clever than her classmates.
commonMistakes.right: She is cleverer than her classmates. / She is more clever than her classmates (both acceptable).
commonMistakes.why: 'Clever' is a two-syllable word that can actually take either form in modern English - 'cleverer' or 'more clever' are both considered acceptable, unlike stricter two-syllable words. This is one of a small group of two-syllable adjectives with flexible comparative forms, so both options work here.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is more quiet than the library.
commonMistakes.right: This is quieter than the library. / This is more quiet than the library (both acceptable).
commonMistakes.why: Similar to 'clever', 'quiet' is another flexible two-syllable adjective where both 'quieter' and 'more quiet' are considered correct in modern usage. Recognize that a handful of two-syllable words allow both forms, unlike the stricter rule for most others.

commonMistakes.wrong: He worked more hard than everyone else.
commonMistakes.right: He worked harder than everyone else.
commonMistakes.why: 'Hard' is a short, one-syllable adverb here, so it takes '-er' directly (harder), not 'more' in front. This same '-er/-est' rule applies to short adverbs, not just adjectives.

commonMistakes.wrong: She arrived more early than expected.
commonMistakes.right: She arrived earlier than expected.
commonMistakes.why: 'Early' follows the two-syllable, y-ending pattern (like happy and easy), becoming 'earlier' directly, not 'more early'. Recognize this pattern even when the word functions as an adverb, not just as an adjective.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the more difficult exam of the year.
commonMistakes.right: This is the most difficult exam of the year.
commonMistakes.why: Since this compares to the WHOLE year (many exams, not just two), the superlative 'most difficult' is needed, not the comparative. Whenever a comparison involves 'of the year', 'in the class', or similar large groups, use the superlative form.

commonMistakes.wrong: Of the two choices, this is the most practical.
commonMistakes.right: Of the two choices, this is the more practical.
commonMistakes.why: Since only two choices are being compared ('of the two'), the comparative 'more practical' is correct, not the superlative 'most practical'. Watch for phrases like 'of the two' or 'between the two', which always signal comparative, not superlative.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is more unique than anything I've seen.
commonMistakes.right: This is more unusual than anything I've seen. / This is unlike anything I've seen.
commonMistakes.why: 'Unique' technically means 'one of a kind', so it can't logically be compared in degrees (something can't be 'more one-of-a-kind') - though this rule is often relaxed in casual speech. For precise, formal writing, avoid comparing 'unique' and use alternative words like 'unusual' or 'rare' instead.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the most perfect solution.
commonMistakes.right: This is the ideal solution. / This is a perfect solution.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'unique', 'perfect' is technically an absolute quality (something is either perfect or it isn't), so adding 'most' doesn't logically work, though it's extremely common in casual speech. For careful writing, avoid intensifying 'perfect' with 'most' or 'more'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is more identical to the original than that one.
commonMistakes.right: This is more similar to the original than that one. / This is identical to the original.
commonMistakes.why: 'Identical' means 'exactly the same', which is also an absolute quality that can't logically have degrees - use 'similar' instead if you want to express varying degrees of closeness. Save 'identical' for describing an exact, complete match, without comparative modifiers.

commonMistakes.wrong: He is the more experienced person in the company.
commonMistakes.right: He is the most experienced person in the company.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'the company' includes many employees (more than two), this needs the superlative 'most experienced', not the comparative. Keep checking group size whenever you use 'the' before a comparative or superlative form.

commonMistakes.wrong: This laptop is more expensive than any laptop.
commonMistakes.right: This laptop is more expensive than any other laptop.
commonMistakes.why: Without the word 'other', this sentence illogically compares the laptop to itself (since 'any laptop' could include this one) - always add 'other' when comparing something to the rest of a group it belongs to. Remember this fix: 'more [adjective] than any other [noun]' is the complete, correct comparative pattern.

commonMistakes.wrong: She is smarter than anyone in her class.
commonMistakes.right: She is smarter than anyone else in her class.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, without 'else', this illogically includes her in the comparison to herself - add 'else' to correctly exclude her from the group she's being compared to. Practice this fixed pattern: 'smarter/taller/faster than anyone else' whenever comparing someone to their own group.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is one of the tallest building in the city.
commonMistakes.right: This is one of the tallest buildings in the city.
commonMistakes.why: The phrase 'one of the' always requires a PLURAL noun afterward (buildings, not building), since you're saying it belongs to a group of several tall buildings. Whenever you use 'one of the [superlative]', make sure the following noun is plural.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is one of the most difficult exam I've taken.
commonMistakes.right: This is one of the most difficult exams I've taken.
commonMistakes.why: Just like the previous example, 'one of the' needs a plural noun afterward (exams), following the same consistent rule. Keep checking this pattern every time you use 'one of the [superlative] [noun]'.

commonMistakes.wrong: The more you practice, the best you become.
commonMistakes.right: The more you practice, the better you become.
commonMistakes.why: In the 'the more...the more' comparative structure, both halves need to use comparative forms consistently (better), not mixing in a superlative (best) partway through. Keep parallel comparative forms throughout this specific sentence pattern: the [comparative], the [comparative].

commonMistakes.wrong: The harder you work, the successfuller you become.
commonMistakes.right: The harder you work, the more successful you become.
commonMistakes.why: Since 'successful' is a long word, it needs 'more' rather than a direct '-er' ending ('successfuller' isn't a word) - apply the long-word rule even within this special comparative structure. Check each half of 'the...the...' sentences individually for the correct comparative form based on word length.

commonMistakes.wrong: This year's sales are more good than last year's.
commonMistakes.right: This year's sales are better than last year's.
commonMistakes.why: This repeats the classic good/better mistake - 'more good' should always become 'better' instead. This irregular form appears so often in daily English that it's worth memorizing completely, without exception.

commonMistakes.wrong: I feel more bad about this than before.
commonMistakes.right: I feel worse about this than before.
commonMistakes.why: Just like 'more good' becomes 'better', 'more bad' should become 'worse' - both follow the same irregular pattern. Keep these two irregular pairs together in memory: good/better, bad/worse.

commonMistakes.wrong: This restaurant is more far from here than that one.
commonMistakes.right: This restaurant is farther from here than that one. / This restaurant is further from here than that one.
commonMistakes.why: 'Far' is irregular and short, becoming either 'farther' (physical distance) or 'further' (figurative distance) - it never takes 'more' in front. Remember this special irregular word: far becomes farther/further, not 'more far'.

commonMistakes.wrong: This is the farrest place I've ever traveled.
commonMistakes.right: This is the farthest place I've ever traveled. / This is the furthest place I've ever traveled.
commonMistakes.why: The superlative of 'far' is 'farthest' or 'furthest', not 'farrest' (which isn't a real word, likely confused with the double-r spelling of similar words). Memorize the complete irregular set: far, farther/further, farthest/furthest.

commonMistakes.wrong: This news is worse than worse.
commonMistakes.right: This news is worse and worse. / This news just keeps getting worse.
commonMistakes.why: 'Worse than worse' isn't a standard phrase - if you want to show something getting progressively more negative, use 'worse and worse' (repeated comparative) instead. Whenever you want to show continuous change, repeat the comparative word joined by 'and', like 'better and better' or 'worse and worse'.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: She is more taller than me.
She is much more taller than me.
quiz.explanation: 'Taller' already has the comparative ending, so 'more' shouldn't be added as well.

quiz.sentence: This is the most best option.
quiz.explanation: 'Best' is already the irregular superlative of 'good' - 'most' is unnecessary.

quiz.question: Choose the correct sentence:
options: He is more intelligenter than his brother.
He is more intelligent than his brother.
He is intelligenter than his brother.
He is most intelligent than his brother.
quiz.answer: He is more intelligent than his brother.
quiz.explanation: Long words like 'intelligent' take 'more/most' alone, without an added ending.

quiz.question: This road is ___ (wide) than that one. (short adjective + er)
Choose the correct sentence:
options: This is one of the tallest building in the city.
This is one of the tallest buildings in the city.
This is one of the taller building in the city.
This is one of tallest building in the city.
quiz.answer: This is one of the tallest buildings in the city.
quiz.explanation: 'One of the' always needs a plural noun afterward.
